clues.clue: This pancreatic hormone lowers blood glucose by helping cells take in sugar.
This simple sugar is the body's main blood-borne fuel.
This organ behind the stomach makes both insulin and digestive enzymes.
The insulin-producing 'beta' ones of these reside in the islets of Langerhans.
This pancreatic hormone opposes insulin by raising blood sugar.
In this form, the body makes little or no insulin; it's often diagnosed in childhood.
This far more common form involves insulin resistance and is strongly tied to lifestyle.
Elevated blood sugar that first appears during pregnancy is this form.
This warning state features higher-than-normal blood sugar that is not yet full diabetes.
When cells stop responding properly to insulin, the body has this 'resistance.'
Excessive thirst and frequent urination are classic early signs of high levels of this.
Long-term high glucose can damage small nerves, causing numbness in the feet; this is peripheral ___.
Diabetes is a leading cause of this damage to the eye's light-sensing retina.
Diabetic kidney damage, a top cause of kidney failure, is this 'nephro-' complication.
A Type 1 emergency of high ketones and blood acid is abbreviated DKA, the 'A' meaning keto-this.
A small device that pricks the finger and reads sugar is this 'glucose' tool.
This blood test, reported as a percentage, reflects average glucose over about three months.
A wearable sensor giving real-time readings is a continuous glucose monitor, abbreviated this.
A 'fasting' glucose test requires going at least this many hours without eating.
Dangerously low blood sugar, bringing shakiness and confusion, is this 'hypo-' condition.
People with Type 1 must take this hormone by injection or pump.
This first-line oral drug for Type 2 mainly cuts the liver's glucose output.
This 'glycemic' ranking measures how quickly a carbohydrate raises blood sugar.
Injectable drugs like semaglutide that curb appetite and sugar are abbreviated GLP-1 ___.
A severe low can be reversed with an emergency injection of this insulin-opposing hormone.

final.clue: This butterfly-shaped neck gland sets the body's metabolic rate via hormones T3 and T4.
